steps.prompt: What kind of clause does 'Si' introduce?

hints.relative clause (who): A relative clause would be introduced by 'qui' — 'si' means 'if'
hints.temporal clause (when): A temporal clause would be introduced by 'cum' or 'quando' — 'si' means 'if'
hints.causal clause (because): A causal clause would be introduced by 'quia' or 'quod' — 'si' means 'if'

hints.to be able: That would be 'posse' — 'vult' comes from 'velle' (to wish/want)
hints.to be willing: Close, but the standard translation of 'velle' is 'to wish/want'
hints.to prefer: That would be 'malle' — 'vult' comes from 'velle' (to wish/want)

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'velit' — 'vult' is present indicative
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'voluit' — 'vult' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'volet' — 'vult' is present indicative

hints.nolo, nolle, nolui, —: These are the parts of 'nolle' (to not wish) — the negative of 'velle'
hints.malo, malle, malui, —: These are the parts of 'malle' (to prefer) — a compound of 'velle'
hints.valeo, valere, valui, valitum: These are the parts of 'valere' (to be strong/well)

steps.prompt: What is the function of 'venire' in this sentence?

hints.purpose infinitive: Purpose is normally expressed by 'ut' + subjunctive — here 'venire' completes 'vult'
hints.subject infinitive: A subject infinitive would be the subject of the sentence — 'venire' completes 'vult'
hints.historical infinitive: Historical infinitive replaces a finite verb in narrative — 'venire' here completes 'vult'

hints.video, videre, vidi, visum: These are the parts of 'videre' (to see)
hints.vivo, vivere, vixi, victum: These are the parts of 'vivere' (to live)
hints.vinco, vincere, vici, victum: These are the parts of 'vincere' (to conquer)

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'ego' — 'me' is accusative after 'post'
hints.ablative: While 'me' looks identical in ablative, 'post' takes accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'mihi' — 'me' is accusative after 'post'

hints.ablative: Some prepositions take ablative, but 'post' always governs the accusative
hints.genitive: Very few prepositions take genitive in Latin — 'post' takes accusative
hints.dative: Prepositions do not take the dative in Latin — 'post' takes accusative

hints.relative pronoun (who): The relative pronoun is 'qui' — 'quis' after 'si' is indefinite
hints.demonstrative pronoun: 'Quis' is not demonstrative; after 'si' it means 'anyone'

translation: If anyone wishes to come after me
